---
title: 'SDS 172: Hello Data!'
author: "YOUR NAME HERE"
output:
  pdf_document: default
  html_document: default
  word_document: default
editor_options:
  chunk_output_type: console
---

# Goals

-   To practice using statistics vocabulary to describe datasets
-   To become familiar with RStudio and be able to knit and save a markdown document.
-   To be able to load and query a dataset using the RStudio Data interface.
-   To use simple functions like `read_csv()`, `dim()` and `colnames()` to interact with a dataset.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button, a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document.

In part 1, we are not going to do any code, we will just modify the text of our document.

## Saving your work

As the instructor, ONLY I can edit and save documents in the `Class` folder. You can create, edit, and save files in your `Student` folder. So before we go any further:

> Save a copy of 01_hello_data.Rmd to your `Student` folder using the **File \> Save As** option.

Only you and the professor have access to this folder; it's the place you should take class notes and work on homework assignments.

# Part 1: Vocabulary

1.  Think about the [Film or Digital](https://pudding.cool/2018/08/filmordigital/) article you read before class. Does this describe an observational study or an experiment?

2.  Which of the following would you consider the response variable: film/digital, year, director, budget, or genre?

3.  In the following list, identify each variable as categorical or numeric:

film/digital

year

director

budget

genre

4.  Click "Help" up top and select "Markdown Quick Reference." Use the example to bold the words "response variable" in #2.

5.  Italicize the name of each variable in #3.

6.  Knit the document as a pdf.

# Part 2: Interacting with Data!

## Data Analysis with tables

Let's start by loading a modified version of the dataset used for one of our first [readings](https://pudding.cool/2018/08/filmordigital/) by using the following R code.

```{r,echo = FALSE, include = FALSE}

library(tidyverse)

movies <- read_csv("~/Sds_172_S26/Class/Data/movies.csv")
```

You can type normally here.

-   The first line tells R that we are going to use all the functions that are in the package `tidyverse`. Inside the package `tidyverse` there is a multitude of useful functions, however in our example we will be only using the function `read_csv()`

-   The second line creates the object `movies` from the output of the function `read_csv`. The function `read_csv` reads a table from a file path (notice how filepaths are enclosed by "") and produces a table or dataset.




## Interacting with the dataset

Notice how when you run this command you will have `movies` in the Environment window (often the upper left side of your screen). If you click on `movies` (**not on the play button**) you will be able to view this dataset similarly to a spreadsheet. In Statistics we refer to the rows of a dataset as *cases* (or *observations*) and to the columns as *variables* (or *features*).

7.  How many cases (movies) are in this dataset?

8.  How many variables are in this dataset?

9.  Find `The Avengers` in this dataset and answer the following questions related to it:

    a.  Who is the director?

    b.  What year did it come out?

    c.  What was the budget?

10. By clicking on each variable (column) you can sort your dataset by that column. Use this method to answer the following questions about our `movies` dataset.

    a.  What is the range of years from the movies in our dataset?

    b.  Which is the most expensive movie? Which one is the cheapest movie?

> Try knitting the document as a pdf again!

It is a good idea to knit often. This makes it easier to figure out where the problem is if you have a mistake.

## Simple functions for querying the dataset

After loading our `movies` dataset we can query our dataset to learn how many cases (rows) and how many variables (columns) we have by using the command `dim` as follows:

```{r}
dim(movies)
```

In this case we have 1120 cases with 6 variables each. We can find the variable names as before using the `Data` environment, but alternatively we can figure them out by using the command `colnames()`:

```{r}
colnames(movies)
```

Notice that most of the names are self-explanatory, however you can find more information about this particular dataset [here](https://github.com/the-pudding/data/tree/master/filmordigital#top_directors_datacsv).

# Conclusions

In this worksheet we have learned the basics of how to interact with Rstudio by being able to knit, load a dataset, and obtain basic information about it.

## A few terms to remember

*R* is the programming language, the code.

*RStudio* is the interface - all the windows and buttons we use to make things go.

Sometimes you'll hear me (or other people) use the words interchangeably, to mean use R to do stuff in RStudio! :)

The gray boxes are called code *chunks*, and everything inside them is *R* code. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

-   Click the "Plus C" button above OR
-   Mac: option + command + I
-   PC: Ctrl + Alt + I

Then this gray box appears and you can type code into it.

```{r}


```

This file is an *R Markdown* document. The symbols you use to change bold, italics, and other formatting are *Markdown* syntax. The end of the file is *.Rmd*, that's how RStudio knows to treat it as an *R Markdown* file. We will always use this type of file in the class. Everything outside the code chunks (not in gray) is text (or formatting symbols).
